segments.text: Hello guys, my name is Terrell Menesys, I work as expert at Dresund Somer in Frankfurt
and I'm in charge of creating tools, development and automation workflows.
Hello and welcome to this session. APS and AI for large-scale project delivery
end-to-end control of your deliverables. The word end-to-end reminds me that last weekend I was a bit
so I thought let's do some adventure and I decided to cook dinner without a recipe so I just
started adding random things, some pasta, some chili, some yogurt, some soy sauce, and in the end
some chocolate. And I ended up creating a publicly new cuisine category. I thought it was really
incredible but in the end it was very inedible. So this sounds silly but unplanned misadventures
like this are really fun in the kitchen but unplanned misadventures like this in an AEC project
can blow off your budget, can be really expensive and can cause a lot of delays.
But still this tends to happen quite often and this is what we want to address. We don't want to
deliver chocolate pasta to our clients so what's the challenge we are facing here?
So AEC projects depend on clearly defined deliverables and yet cleverties often missing. We are often
faced with questions like what are we actually delivering? Who is supposed to deliver it? Who is
in charge? And where do we stand right now? What's the status? Are the deliverables issued, submitted
already, and are the deliverables compliant? Do they follow the guidelines? Now imagine a large-scale
AEC project. It often enoules more than 11 different disciplines. We will be working on
30 different models and in the end how to deliver more than 6000 deliverables. And what's typically
delivered? So typical deliverables consist of drawings like floor plan sections, elevations,
and also technical data sheets like reports, calculations, etc. And most of these come from
3D models. And as you see two-thirds of these deliverables are drawings and that's where the
money is. And this is what we aim to track in the beginning. How do we do this so far?
Traditionally this was a three-step process. Define, create, and deliver. Sounds really easy,
so deliverables were defined in excel sheet, a massive excel list. And then someone had to go through
this excel and create a responding VEWS and sheets in the Revit model. And then in the end
export these VEWS and sheets from the Revit model with the required naming convention to a
designated folder on Autodesk format, formerly known as Autodesk Construction Cloud.
But there are some challenges here. What are the key challenges? Disconnected data.
Excel file is not optimal for collaborative working. It's a static snapshot in time.
When lots of people are saving different versions, working on different versions, nobody knows
which is the latest file. And also there is no connection to the source models.
So we cannot know the status. And then there is lack of transparency.
It is not clear who is in charge of which is deliverable. And also the status is not visible.
And then in the end people had to manually go through the sheets and check for quality.
And then export all of the sheets and leaves manually. So that's a lot of repetitive,
intensive boring work. This is a recipe for disaster. And in this setting people tend to
often delay the tasks until the end. And then when the deadline is approaching, there is a lot
of panic. And we don't want to do all the work while crying. That's why two years ago we came up with
the roadmap to have a unified solution for this issue. And using Autodesk Platform services,
we managed to achieve most of these milestones, but still there is a lot more to go.
So basically we wanted to centralize our deliverables instead of storing it in a local file.
Then we wanted to establish connection to the source models and deliverables.
We wanted to check quality of all the sheets based on design guidelines.
And we also wanted to automate the exports.
And then we wanted to try all of the solutions as the proof of concept on a real project.
And then we also wanted to automatically create missing sheets.
And in the end implement AI for insights and prediction.
When we started working, we started right in the middle. Because it was the easiest thing to start with.
We introduced a technical setup behind this in the last year's DevCon.
And now we will show you what we are added on top. But before we move on, I will give you a brief
overview on how our deliverables are structured. So a set of parameters are initially defined for
a viewing project like this. In a simple way, three tables, you can see here the different values,
there are codified values on left and there's description on the right.
So a simple floor plan created for the first floor Brian architect would be codified like this.
Right, that's how we derive the file name for the documents. And then based on this logic,
the list would look like this. This is a very, very simple example. Now back to our roadline,
centralization. What are we solving here? We need a centralized way to define deliverables.
We need scopes for clear ownership. We want to have a configurable file name in convention.
So we implemented our solution using MongoDB as a database in our tool called I love the
L. The L stands for deliverables and we love them of course. And as you can see in our world map,
we also wanted to try the solution in real use case. And that's what we did this year. We implemented
this on one of our own project, which is let's say an experimental office building.
And for this project, we tried defining our deliverables in the newly implemented solution.
So here you can see the parameters like building, trade, document type are clearly defined
in the interface and then combination of these parameters.
You can see here in the master deliverable list. Here each row is supposed to represent one
deliverable. Here you can also see the file name. For the file name in convention,
we used previously a very complex Excel formula. It looks really, really complex that only
Neo could read it, but we don't have Neo in our theme. So we don't want that. So we developed an
easily configurable interface to set up the naming convention. The code was to have an
adjustable system instead of having static formula that's relative to columns. If naming convention
had to be changed and there were extra columns in Excel, people ended up creating the whole list
from scratch because it was too complex. But with this new approach, it's just as easy as moving
the bubbles here. And as we upload the deliverables to Autodest Forma, we need to have this exact
naming convention also on Autodest Form. But currently we don't have a way to do this via API,
so we use the Excel import option on Forma. So now we have defined our deliverables.
centrally, we still have an issue ownership out of 6000 deliverables. How does someone know
which documents they're in charge of creating? This reminds me that I used to be part of a huge
WhatsApp family group, which had 70 members in it. And every morning I wake up, there used to be
999 hundred messages. So one day I decided to open it. What's going on there? When I opened,
there were so many messages and then I saw there was a message from my mom. She asked me to buy some
onions. So I went out and bought a lot of onions. And then I realized it wasn't my mom. It was someone
else's mom. It was just a user name. And I also realized that I live in Germany now and my mom
is in India. So what do I do with all these onions? What I'm trying to say here is our lives will be
much simpler if we only see the data that we are supposed to see and not everything all at once.
And how did we do this in our tool before? We had Excel groups. And the users had to navigate
these groups on their own and just find out stuff. So basically for me this looks like a one
bit giant WhatsApp family group. And we don't want this. That's why we decided to define scopes
for roads based on parameters. In this example,
based on trade and area designation, in area work, we have seven scopes defined.
And based on these scopes, we can filter our master list and show only relevant data for user
based on their roads. But what's still missing here is the connection to rev it models.
That brings us to our next step on the roadmap connection to models.
So for use case we have seven different models on former cloud, which we must link to our scopes.
For this we use AEC data model API to query element groups. And then from the current results,
we get the IDs of the element groups and we link map these IDs to our scopes.
You can also see that we can also get this alternative identifiers, which we can use with the
data management API to get some metadata from this file item. For example,
for structuring project ID and model ID, we can use this to automatically edit the cloud model
using automation API and synchronize and stuff like this. So with this data, we provide the users
a drop down to easily map the files to the scopes and the background, we link to element group
by here. We can also map one relevant model to multiple scopes, just in case users are working
on fragmented files, which are linked to one big model, it's also possible to map one model to
multiple scopes. But there's still something missing. How would we know which sheet or
wave inside relevant maps to which deliverable? For this we need an element level connection.
How do we do this? So our defined deliverable list has parameters.
And also sheets inside relevant model have parameters and we have to map these.
And we are not planning to read out these parameters using the OCR functionality,
but we once again use AEC data model API. We know which relevant model we must check from the scopes,
so basically we query the relevant sheets from this model and also the project information,
along with their parameters, a parameter needs. Now we have the names of all the parameters,
which we can let the users using a drop down to map to the parameters in our deliverable list.
So all of the setup we did until now can be saved as a template, so the users can reuse it
for a new project if we tell the similar structure, they don't have to do it all over again.
So with this we can say the models are fully connected to the deliverables.
And based on this connection we have a comparison page in our tool which shows us the current status
of the deliverables in the models. Here we can see a comparison window which shows current status
of the model for architectures code. The values here are first filtered based on relevant documents
like floor plans sections, etc. and not the technical data sheets for now. The green dot here

Yellow dot means the sheet exists in brevity, but all the parameters are not matching,

And what do we do with the sheets whose parameters don't match like this? So for this you can
click the button at the bottom in our tool called fixinbrevity and then once you click it,
tasks would be created in the tool. Here you can see the names of the files for which tasks have

Right and what happens once they execute it what happens in the backrop? So once again we have
this information from our scopes that we derived earlier and we also have the inputs.
So we use automation API to send these inputs as a work item. Basically we open the
private cloud model and then we assign the right values to the parameters using the inputs
and then we synchronize the model. So now the parameters are fixed but do we still have everything
we need? Just having the parameters right is this enough? What if the sheet is empty or what if
wrong title block was used or wrong report was used? Could we export the sheet and submit it to
the clients? What about the contents? For this we need a quality control and this brings us to
the next step design guidelines. So we need to establish some guidelines which we can use to
check qualities of the sheet in our model. So this is how we define guidelines for our projects
for different types of plans. For example what kinds of legends and notations reports it should
have, how the dimensions have to be marked and so on. These are some guidelines defined for our
case study project and we configured these guidelines using our relic plugin colidious tool
which was presented at 824 other days in university when working on models this tool gives
users feedback on what is wrong and what to do next. And in this tool we have user interface where
we can create design guidelines configurations like what title block to use, what legend, what
report and lots of other options and then we save these settings in the data storage object
after a relic model. And how do we access it from here? We once again use automation API.
We know which relic model to work on. We just open this model on the cloud, we extract the design
guidelines from the data storage object, then we check all the sheets if they comply and then we
send this information back to the tool. And with this information you can show an additional column
in our comparison window as a percentage of compliance. So basically here you can see three out of
nine sheets past the guideline checks and they are good to go. So we can export them and deliver
them. But once again we don't want to export them manually because this can take really long time
with lots of the lurables. This brings us to our next step automating exports. As I mentioned
earlier this was the first thing that we saw because it was easiest to implement. Our setup
looked like this. So the users can easily configure export settings in our tool for a relic file on

used and then using automation API or Revit. The model is open on the cloud and deliverables,
views and sheets are exported and uploaded to a designated folder on our last format.
With this we have the full cycle in line from defining the deliverables to export them.
So what's next? The next step is AI implementation. Now why do we need this? Is this because everybody
else is implementing AI? Now imagine it's Friday night and you open Netflix, you want to
watch something funny so you filter comedy moves. But there are still thousands of titles,
you keep scrolling and scrolling and you finish your popcorn bucket and 20 meatballs but you
still didn't find the movie. So next time you use AI assistant, you ask I want something funny
under 90 minutes with Adam Sandler and this turns an overwhelming choice into action-able
recommendations. In our use case, our comparison window shows us this status. But the AI
assistants can refine the choices and give simple summaries and actionable insights for the user.
So we have implemented a simple AI assist layer which will be improved in the upcoming days
but the beginning with here's how it works. For this we use open AI model GPT-4 or many.
Imagine the user asks what details are missing in the electrical scope.
The question is sent to the AI model to parse the intent of the question first because if the user
asks something irrelevant then it shouldn't try to answer it. Once the intent is parsed we know
what scope the user is talking about and what document type the user is asking about and then we also
know he's talking about missing sheets. So based on this intent we filter data in our tool,
we gather facts and then we send these gathered facts back to the model to analyze
based on the analysis provide an output and we display this output to the users along with
actionable insights if available. Here you can see it in action. The user is asking for floor
plans that need review in architecture scope. Then we get the result with some statistics,
with some numbers, also the top mismatched parameters. Then at the bottom you can see two buttons
which are actionable insights. Now the user can click filter to filter the sheets based on the
result and then can just review the sheets one by one, check the missing parameters and probably
fix them one by one or the user can use the other actionable insight button. So it creates
the automation tasks for all of the sheets at once like this. Basically with this we as of
today are using AI just for assistance for quick summary, quick insights but we aim to connect data
in future and we can use this data to learn patterns which can be analyzed by AI to predict certain
outcomes and based on these predictions we can make smart decisions on our upcoming project.
Basically in the end we need something like a predictive and generative AI which means
we would like to automatically generate deliverables using AI based on user inputs. For example what
kind of building we are working on like airport. Number of floors involved, number of traits and
based on this input it should automatically create the deliverables content and then we also want to
detect patterns based on past projects and warn the users early. We also want to predict readiness
risk and also warn the users early before milestones. This is our next steps, future outlook and in
addition we also want to implement automatic creations of missing sheets based on guidelines.
It was also in our milestones but we did not achieve it yet. We also want to
you cross the citizen checks for example check compared the architecture model with the fire

this? Why do we have such a big pipeline set up? Compute this manually. So have you ever
tried redlining 6000 documents overnight and made it out alive?
This guy did and he's still 21 years old. What I'm trying to say is if you spend hours and hours
on repetitive tasks it's a waste of human intelligence. It don't want to be the architect who is
stressed who has to go to 6000 sheets manually to check if they're up to quality and export them
and print them at midnight no matter how hard it tries. It's not going to make it.
But if most of the boring work labor intense and repetitive work was automated
then you can concentrate on things that need human intelligence. And thanks to Autodesk
Platform Services and AI we can make this possible. My name is Devo thank you very much for listening.
